Party_Details.Name: Long Say Ting Daniel

Facts.Text: From July 2005, Daniel Long knew Dr Jusuf Merukh and served as his Mercedes limousine driver during the latter's Singapore business trips.
In June 2007, Daniel Long assisted Dr Jusuf Merukh in sourcing for and buying condominium units in Singapore.
On 30 April 2010, Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd was incorporated with Daniel Long and Dr Jusuf Merukh as directors and Dr Jusuf Merukh as the sole shareholder.
On 30 April 2010, Daniel Long was a director of Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd.
Daniel Long incorporated a total of 14 companies in Singapore which were all wholly owned by Dr Jusuf Merukh.
At its incorporation, the company’s business was described as an 'investment company'.
From 30 April 2010 until 30 April 2011, the company drew no income while incurring expenses of $1,424,377.00, most of which went towards acquiring various properties.
All acquisitions made by the company were funded entirely through transfers from Dr Jusuf Merukh’s bank account in Indonesia.
Dr Jusuf Merukh gave Daniel Long a Power of Attorney to manage and carry out his business transactions, and made him the local representative for the various business ventures in Singapore.
The company embarked on the purchase of seven more condominium units, but these purchases were subsequently aborted due to fund transfer problems from Indonesia.

The company owned three properties: the Bayshore property at Blk 72 Bayshore Road #29-16 Costa Del Sol, the Kitchener property at 10 Kitchener Link #13-18 City Square Residences, and The Raintree at 87 Bukit Drive #06-18 The Raintree.

The company paid for the rental of the church Dr Jusuf Merukh worshipped at in Singapore, purchased a fleet of Rolls Royce, BMW and Mercedes Benz motorcars and hired chauffeurs to provide transport for his family, staff, business associates, and pastors of his church.
Daniel Long became the company’s full time local director despite having no prior business experience.
Daniel Long was the co-local director of Merukh Republic Auto Pte Ltd with Mr Tan Kim Huat, and was also the personal guarantor for Merukh Auto’s debts.
On 22 June 2011, Dr Jusuf Merukh died, after which Daniel Long was the sole director of the company.
After the sole shareholder's death, the company fell behind on various obligations such as payment of staff salaries, as well as the monthly loan instalments for the three properties.
On 27 June 2011, Daniel Long sent a text to Rudolf Johanes Merukh requesting a convenient time to meet up and discuss the company’s operations and properties.
On 5 July 2011, Rudy and Daniel Long exchanged a phone call and texts regarding the company’s financial status and obligations.

On 5 July 2011, Daniel Long responded to Rudy’s request for an email update, detailing the company’s operating costs and outstanding payments which included staff salary with CPF contributions, the mortgage loans for the three properties amounting to $19,331.62 in total, and the outstanding $2,000,864.89 needed to complete the purchase of another condominium unit.

On 8 July 2011, further emails were sent by Daniel Long or by the company’s staff to either Rudy or one of the deceased’s business representatives in Indonesia, appealing for funds with increasing urgency.
On 14 July 2011, further emails were sent appealing for funds with increasing urgency.
On 19 July 2011, further emails were sent appealing for funds with increasing urgency.
On 21 July 2011, further emails were sent appealing for funds with increasing urgency.
On 22 July 2011, the seller of the uncompleted property issued a 21 days’ notice instructing completion.
That notice eventually lapsed and the sale was aborted.

On 28 July 2011, the company’s staff in Singapore followed up with an email to Rudy, requesting the executor of the Estate to sign an attached copy of the Company’s board circular resolution to sell off all properties and cars owned by the Company and the other Merukh Companies, settle all debts, and authorise closure or disposal of all Merukh Companies registered in Singapore.
Sometime in July 2011, the creditors who had financed Merukh Auto’s car purchases called to repossess the cars that had been purchased for the use of various pastors.
As personal guarantor, Daniel Long faced a claim from one of the creditors, Motor-Way, for the shortfall caused by the depreciation of the repossessed cars.
The salaries and CPF contributions of the staff of the Company and of Merukh Auto, including that of all the hired chauffeurs, remained outstanding.
After a series of unheeded warnings threatening penalties and criminal sanction throughout the months of July and August 2011, a charge under s 7(1) of the CPF Act was issued against Merukh Auto.
On 20 August 2011, UOB issued a final reminder accompanied by a threat of legal action.

Beginning with an email sent on 5 July 2011, Daniel Long repeatedly appealed to Rudy for the transfer of funds to meet the instalment payments, warning that an auction sale of the Three Properties would probably result in a poor sales price and losses to the Company.

On 18 July 2011, Rudy and Daniel Long had a phone conversation in which Rudy instructed that the Merukh family had decided to sell all the Three Properties and cars in Singapore, and to wind down the Singapore companies under Daniel Long’s charge.
On 20 July 2011, Rudy’s text to Daniel Long confirmed the instruction to sell all the Three Properties and cars in Singapore and to wind down the Singapore companies.

On 3 August 2011, Daniel Long informed Rudy via email that he had appointed a property agent to market the Three Properties, and warned Rudy of the need to sell quickly in order to avoid repossession by the bank and an auction sale which would likely yield an unsatisfactory price.
On 3 August 2011, Daniel Long appointed a property agent to market the Three Properties.
Motor-Way had made offers for the Bayshore and Kitchener properties of $1,800,000 and $1,500,000 respectively, which were slightly below the purchase prices and current market prices.

Daniel Long explained in the email that the property market was weak due to anticipated government cooling measures and the instability of the financial markets, and requested for Rudy to comment on the offer.

Further emails were sent by the company’s staff to Rudy on 15 August 2011, urgently appealing for the Board Resolution to be signed and for funds to be transferred so as to meet the staff salary and CPF payments which now totalled $117,982.
Further emails were sent by the company’s staff to Rudy on 17 August 2011, urgently appealing for the Board Resolution to be signed and for funds.
Further emails were sent by the company’s staff to Rudy on 22 August 2011, urgently appealing for the Board Resolution to be signed and for funds.
Further emails were sent by the company’s staff to Rudy on 25 August 2011, urgently appealing for the Board Resolution to be signed and for funds.

On 25 August 2011, Daniel Long sent Rudy a formal letter stating that he would be striking off all the solvent Merukh Companies in Singapore shortly, and hoped to do the same with Merukh Auto and the Company once their outstanding loan and credit issues had been resolved.

Daniel Long stated that he was trying his best to get a buyer for at least one of the three properties so as to raise some funds to meet the various pressing obligations, but that the market was slow and no offers were presently forthcoming.

Daniel Long emailed Rudy a copy of UOB’s final reminder dated 20 August 2011, as well as a letter issued by Motor-Way’s lawyers claiming against Daniel Long as guarantor for Motor-Way’s outstanding debts.
Further emails were sent by the company’s staff to Rudy on 30 August 2011, urgently appealing for the Board Resolution to be signed and for funds.

The final email before the granting of options to purchase for the Three Properties began was dated 31 August 2011. Daniel Long expressed that more than a week had passed since his email to Rudy pertaining to the outstanding employees’ salaries and the required documentation to sell off the Three Properties and the deceased’s Toyota Wish car.
On 2 September 2011, Daniel Long received an offer for the Bayshore property which exceeded the original purchase price.
He emailed Rudy to inform him that he planned to grant the option to purchase.
Daniel Long granted the option to purchase for the Bayshore property on 5 September 2011.
Daniel Long then went on to grant an option to purchase for the Kitchener property the next day.
Daniel Long promptly emailed Rudy attaching a copy of both of these options.

On 8 September 2011, the estate representative wrote to Daniel Long stating that the Estate did not want the Three Properties to be sold for less than $2,000,000, $1,700,000 and $1,200,000 respectively.
Daniel Long went on to grant the option to purchase The Raintree for a sum $10,000 less than the minimum stated price the following day.
The sale prices obtained by Daniel Long for the Three Properties were $1,917,500, $1,551,000 and $1,190,000 for the Bayshore property, Kitchener property and The Raintree respectively.
The sale prices were $1,917,500, $1,551,000 and $1,190,000, and the original purchase prices were $1,916,200, $1,550,000 and $1,155,000 respectively.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a sole director who sells company properties under severe financial pressure following the sole shareholder's death breaches his duties by failing to obtain formal general meeting approval, and whether he ought fairly to be excused.
Party_Details.Rule: Section 160 of the Companies Act requires directors to obtain approval in general meeting before carrying into effect proposals for disposing of the whole or substantially the whole of the company’s undertaking or property. However, under section 391 of the Companies Act, a court may relieve a company officer from liability for negligence, default, breach of duty or breach of trust if the officer acted honestly and reasonably and ought fairly to be excused.
Party_Details.Application: Daniel Long, as sole director after 22 June 2011, managed the company in a context of unpaid staff salaries, mortgage arrears, and creditor pressure, while repeatedly seeking instructions and funds from the deceased’s family. He obtained informal instructions from a family member to sell the properties and wind down the companies. Facing a final reminder from the mortgagee bank and continuing lack of funds, he granted options to purchase the three properties in early September 2011 at prices above their original purchase prices, before receiving a later communication from the estate specifying higher minimum desired prices. The estate subsequently challenged his authority to complete the sales without formal shareholder approval.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that the plaintiff’s conduct in granting the options and effecting the sales was not motivated by personal gain and occurred against a background of persistent efforts to obtain instructions and approvals from the deceased’s family. The judge accepted that the plaintiff acted honestly and that his failure to obtain prior general meeting approval was a genuine oversight. In assessing reasonableness, the judge took into account the plaintiff’s lack of business experience, the financial pressures from mortgage arrears and creditor claims, and the fact that the achieved sale prices were close to independent valuations and above original purchase prices. The judge concluded that the plaintiff acted as he would have in his own affairs and that his conduct was highly reasonable. Therefore, it was fair to excuse the plaintiff from liability to the company for the procedural breaches.

Party_Details.Name: Merukh Nunik Elizabeth (personal representative of the estate of Merukh Jusuf, deceased)

Facts.Text: On 22 June 2011, Dr Jusuf Merukh died, and his daughter Nunik Elizabeth Merukh acted as personal representative of his estate.

By 30 April 2010, Dr Jusuf Merukh was the sole shareholder of Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd and had provided all funds for the purchase of the Three Properties through transfers from his Indonesian bank account.
By 30 April 2010, Dr Jusuf Merukh had incorporated 14 companies in Singapore, each wholly owned by him, including Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd.

On 18 July 2011, during a phone conversation with Daniel Long, Rudolf Johanes Merukh, the eldest son of the deceased, stated that the Merukh family had decided to sell all Three Properties and cars in Singapore and to wind down the Singapore companies under Daniel Long’s charge.
On 20 July 2011, Rudolf Johanes Merukh sent a text message to Daniel Long confirming the family’s decision to sell all Three Properties and cars in Singapore and to wind down the Singapore companies.

On 28 July 2011, Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd staff emailed Rudolf Johanes Merukh requesting that the executor of the estate sign a board circular resolution authorising the sale of all properties and cars owned by Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd and other Merukh companies, settlement of all debts, and closure or disposal of all Merukh companies in Singapore.
On 3 August 2011, Nunik Elizabeth Merukh requested and obtained from United Overseas Bank a six-month grace period for payment of outstanding instalments on the Three Properties.

On 8 September 2011, Nunik Elizabeth Merukh wrote to Daniel Long stating that the estate did not want the Bayshore property sold for less than $2,000,000, the Kitchener property for less than $1,700,000, and The Raintree for less than $1,200,000.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the estate of a deceased sole shareholder can hold a director personally liable for unapproved property sales when the director acted to avert financial collapse, and whether statutory relief blocks all third-party claims.
Party_Details.Rule: Section 391 of the Companies Act provides for court-ordered relief from liability for company officers in proceedings for negligence, default, breach of duty or trust, but its scope is confined to actions brought by the company and does not shield officers from direct third-party claims or personal actions by an estate.
Party_Details.Application: The estate communicated desired minimum sale prices to the director after he had already granted options to purchase the properties. The estate's representatives subsequently challenged the director's authority to sell the properties without formal corporate approval and below the estate's stated minimum prices. The estate argued that the director should not be granted a blanket exemption from liability, preserving its right to pursue separate actions against him.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge examined whether section 391 of the Companies Act could extend to proceedings brought by persons other than the company, such as the estate. After analysing case law and the legislative history, the judge concluded that section 391 operates as a balancing mechanism within company law and is confined to liabilities owed in relation to the company. The judge held that relief is not available against third-party claims and that section 391 does not apply to actions brought by the estate or its representative. Therefore, while the court granted relief to the director concerning potential claims by the company itself, it declined to grant prospective relief to block potential direct claims by the estate.

Party_Details.Name: Motor-Way Credit Pte Ltd (intervener)

Facts.Text: Sometime in July 2011, the creditors who had financed Merukh Auto’s car purchases called to repossess the cars that had been purchased for the use of various pastors.
Sometime in July 2011, Daniel Long faced a claim from one of the creditors, Motor-Way Credit Pte Ltd, for the shortfall caused by the depreciation of the repossessed cars.

On 25 August 2011, a formal letter was sent stating that the solvent Merukh Companies in Singapore would be struck off shortly, and that the same would be done with Merukh Auto and the Company once their outstanding loan and credit issues had been resolved.
On 3 August 2011, Motor-Way had made offers for the Bayshore property of $1,800,000 and the Kitchener property of $1,500,000.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a creditor's pursuit of a personal guarantee and concurrent offers to purchase company assets contribute to the financial exigency justifying a director's emergency asset sale.
Party_Details.Rule: Creditor pressure, including the enforcement of personal guarantees and the threat of foreclosure, is a relevant factual circumstance when evaluating whether a director acted reasonably in liquidating company assets without formal shareholder approval.
Party_Details.Application: Motor-Way Credit Pte Ltd, as a creditor of Merukh Republic Auto Pte Ltd, repossessed vehicles and pursued Daniel Long under his personal guarantee for the shortfall. Simultaneously, it made purchase offers for the Bayshore and Kitchener properties at prices slightly below their original purchase and market values. These creditor actions and offers formed part of the financial pressures considered by Daniel Long when deciding whether and how quickly to sell the properties.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court treated Motor-Way Credit Pte Ltd’s role primarily as part of the factual background illustrating the financial pressures on Daniel Long. The court did not focus on independent liability issues concerning Motor-Way, but rather considered its demands for payment and its discounted property offers as relevant context. This context substantiated the director's reasonable belief that an immediate sale on the open market was necessary to protect the company's remaining equity from distressed liquidation.
